description: Sync the build_plugin MCP prompt and plugin-development MCP resource with new gotchas #28-29, #31 from the build-plugin skill. The skill was updated with learnings from the Gmail plugin build: when one API path is blocked, explore extension APIs (gmonkey, webpackChunk); web apps expose programmatic internal APIs; internal API endpoints can be deprecated without warning. Prior PRD applied gotchas 1-28 (old version). This PRD covers the delta.

userStories.title: Update prompt gotcha #18 and add gotchas #19-20 from new skill learnings
userStories.description: The buildPluginPromptText's gotcha #18 says 'Write operations may silently fail without cryptographic tokens — omit the tool'. The skill's gotcha #28 was rewritten to instead say 'When one API path is blocked, find another — explore extension APIs, internal endpoints'. Also add two new gotchas from skill #29 and #31.
acceptanceCriteria: Gotcha #18 is rewritten from 'omit the tool' to 'explore extension APIs, alternative internal endpoints, or higher-level programmatic interfaces before giving up'
A new gotcha #19 is added: 'Web apps expose programmatic extension APIs on the page — check for non-standard window globals that provide higher-level operations than raw XHR'
A new gotcha #20 is added: 'Internal API endpoints can be deprecated without warning — test each independently and remove tools that depend on deprecated endpoints'
Total gotchas in the prompt is now 20
userStories.notes:
Files:
- platform/mcp-server/src/mcp-prompts.ts (modify buildPluginPromptText Common Gotchas section, around lines 526-545)

Context:
The current gotcha #18 at approximately line 545 reads:
`18. **Write operations may silently fail without cryptographic tokens** — if a write returns 200 but the action doesn't take effect, the API likely requires an additional cryptographic payload. Omit the tool rather than shipping one that silently fails.`

The updated skill gotcha #28 reads:
`28. **When one API path is blocked, find another** — If a write operation requires an undocumented cryptographic payload that can't be replicated (e.g., Gmail's sync API send token), don't give up. Explore the web app's internal extension APIs (e.g., Gmail's window.gmonkey), JavaScript-exposed compose/send infrastructure, or other internal endpoints. Web apps that are complex enough to have obfuscated crypto tokens usually also expose higher-level programmatic interfaces for extensions, accessibility, or testing. Use browser_execute_script to enumerate page globals, check for extension APIs (gmonkey, __NEXT_DATA__, webpackChunk), and test them interactively.`

Changes:
1. Rewrite gotcha #18 to: `18. **When one API path is blocked, find another** — if a write operation requires an undocumented cryptographic payload, don't give up. Explore the web app's internal extension APIs, JavaScript-exposed programmatic interfaces, or other internal endpoints. Complex apps usually expose higher-level APIs for extensions/accessibility. Use \`browser_execute_script\` to enumerate non-standard page globals.`

2. Add gotcha #19: `19. **Web apps expose programmatic extension APIs on the page** — complex web apps often expose internal scripting APIs on \`window\` that provide higher-level operations than raw XHR endpoints. Discovery: use \`browser_execute_script\` with \`Object.keys(window).filter(...)\` to find non-standard globals, then explore their methods.`

3. Add gotcha #20: `20. **Internal API endpoints can be deprecated without warning** — when building plugins for web apps with multiple API generations, test each endpoint independently. If an endpoint returns 404 or 403, it may be deprecated for that account or region. Remove tools that depend on deprecated endpoints rather than shipping broken tools.`

Constraints:
- Do NOT include Gmail-specific details (gmonkey compose polling, Pk.isEnabled, etc.) — that's too app-specific
- Do NOT mention Gmail by name — keep gotchas generic
- Keep each gotcha to 1-3 lines

userStories.title: Add extension API discovery and API deprecation patterns to plugin-development resource
userStories.description: The opentabs://guide/plugin-development resource's Advanced Auth Patterns section should mention extension API discovery as an alternative when standard API paths are blocked. Also add a note about internal API deprecation.
acceptanceCriteria: The Advanced Auth Patterns section or a new subsection mentions that complex web apps may expose programmatic extension APIs on window
Mentions using browser_execute_script to enumerate non-standard globals
Mentions that internal API endpoints can be deprecated without warning and should be tested independently
Does NOT include Gmail-specific details (gmonkey, GmailDraftMessage, etc.)
userStories.notes:
Files:
- platform/mcp-server/src/mcp-resources.ts (modify PLUGIN_DEVELOPMENT_CONTENT, add to Advanced Auth Patterns section)

Context:
The resource already has an 'Advanced Auth Patterns' section (around line 800) with subsections for XHR/Fetch Interception, Cookie-Based Auth with CSRF, and Opaque Auth Headers. Add a new subsection after Opaque Auth Headers.

Add:

### Extension/Programmatic APIs

When standard API paths are blocked (undocumented crypto tokens, deprecated endpoints), complex web apps often expose higher-level programmatic interfaces:

- Internal extension APIs on `window` (compose, send, draft management)
- JavaScript-exposed infrastructure for accessibility or testing
- `webpackChunk`-based module access to internal stores

Discovery: use `browser_execute_script` with `Object.keys(window).filter(k => !['location','chrome',...].includes(k))` to find non-standard globals, then explore their methods.

### API Deprecation

Internal API endpoints can be deprecated without warning. When multiple API generations exist, test each endpoint independently. If an endpoint returns 404 or 403 unexpectedly, it may be deprecated for that account. Remove tools that depend on deprecated endpoints.

Constraints:
- Keep additions to ~15-20 lines total
- Do NOT mention Gmail, gmonkey, or any app-specific details
- Place the new subsections after the existing 'Opaque Auth Headers' subsection and before the 'CSP Considerations' section
